disease.name: Achalasia Cardia
disease.note: Failure of LES relaxation due to loss of inhibitory neurons in the esophageal myenteric plexus. Classic triad: dysphagia to both solids and liquids, regurgitation of undigested food, and retrosternal chest pain. Bird-beak appearance on barium swallow is pathognomonic. In East Africa, Chagas disease must be considered in endemic areas.
presentingComplaints: Difficulty swallowing both solids and liquids
Food coming back up when lying down
Weight loss from fear of eating
pathophysiologyDetail: Selective loss of inhibitory nitrergic (NOS-positive) neurons in the esophageal myenteric (Auerbach's) plexus with relative preservation of cholinergic excitatory neurons
Autoimmune-mediated destruction: anti-myenteric neuronal antibodies (anti-Hu, anti-neuronal) found in 50-70% of patients, targeting type I S-nitrosoglutathione reductase
Genetic susceptibility: HLA-DQw1, HLA-DRw52a associations in European populations; familial clustering in 2-5% with ALADIN gene mutations in Allgrove syndrome (triple-A syndrome)
Viral trigger hypothesis: HSV-1, measles, and varicella zoster antigens detected in myenteric plexus; molecular mimicry with host neuronal antigens proposed
Impaired nitric oxide (NO) synthase activity results in failure of inhibitory junction potentials and absence of LES relaxation upon swallowing
Loss of interstitial cells of Cajal (ICC) in the lower esophageal sphincter region, disrupts the NO signaling cascade between neurons and smooth muscle
Chagas disease (Trypanosoma cruzi) causes a similar pathology via destruction of the myenteric plexus in endemic regions of East Africa, South and Central America
Progressive aperistalsis of the esophageal body: failed peristaltic propagation in the distal two-thirds with preserved proximal striated muscle function
Functional obstruction at the gastroesophageal junction leads to esophageal stasis, fermentation, and chronic inflammation
Elevated intraesophageal pressure triggers progressive dilatation from fusiform to sigmoid (end-stage) morphology
Chronic stasis esophagitis with squamous hyperplasia, lymphocytic infiltration, and increased risk of squamous cell carcinoma (16-33x relative risk)
Abnormal calcium sensitization pathways in LES smooth muscle involving Rho-kinase activation contributes to the hypertensive sphincter

landmarkTrials.trial: European Multicenter Trial of Pneumatic Dilation vs Laparoscopic Heller Myotomy
landmarkTrials.population: 201 patients with newly diagnosed achalasia across 14 European centers
landmarkTrials.finding: No significant difference in success rate at 2 years between pneumatic dilation (90%) and laparoscopic Heller myotomy (93%); 25% of dilation patients required repeat dilation
landmarkTrials.impact: Established equivalence of primary treatment options; recommended shared decision-making based on patient preference and center expertise
landmarkTrials.reference: Boeckxstaens GE, Annis G, Des Varannes SB, et al. Pneumatic dilation versus laparoscopic Heller's myotomy for idiopathic achalasia. N Engl J Med. 2011;364(19):1807-1816.

landmarkTrials.trial: POEM vs Laparoscopic Heller Myotomy with Fundoplication (POEM-LHM Trial)
landmarkTrials.population: 133 patients with type I-III achalasia, randomized to POEM vs LHM with Dor fundoplication
landmarkTrials.finding: POEM non-inferior to LHM at 2 years (Eckardt score ≤3: 90.6% vs 93.9%); POEM had higher GERD rates (44% vs 29% on pH monitoring)
landmarkTrials.impact: POEM is an effective alternative but with higher post-procedure GERD, reinforcing need for careful patient selection and post-POEM surveillance
landmarkTrials.reference: Werner YB, Hakanson B, Martinek J, et al. Endoscopic or surgical myotomy in patients with idiopathic achalasia. N Engl J Med. 2019;381(23):2219-2229.

landmarkTrials.trial: Randomized Trial of POEM vs Pneumatic Dilation
landmarkTrials.finding: POEM superior to pneumatic dilation at 2 years (92% vs 54% success; Eckardt score ≤3); quality of life scores better in POEM group
landmarkTrials.impact: Challenged the role of pneumatic dilation as first-line therapy in fit patients; POEM increasingly considered definitive treatment option
landmarkTrials.reference: Ponds FA, Fockens P, Lei A, et al. Effect of peroral endoscopic myotomy vs pneumatic dilation on symptom severity and treatment outcomes among treatment-naive patients with achalasia. JAMA. 2019;322(2):134-144.

landmarkTrials.trial: Long-term Outcomes After Heller Myotomy (Mayo Clinic Series)
landmarkTrials.population: 610 patients undergoing Heller myotomy over 30 years
landmarkTrials.finding: Dysphagia improved in 89% at 1 year, 80% at 5 years, 72% at 10 years; reflux symptoms in 28% with fundoplication vs 41% without
landmarkTrials.impact: Long-term durability confirmed; fundoplication should be routinely added to myotomy
landmarkTrials.reference: Greene CL, Chang EJ, Oh DS, et al. Long-term outcomes of laparoscopic Heller myotomy with Dor fundoplication. J Am Coll Surg. 2012;215(5):644-649.

landmarkTrials.population: 527 patients managed with pneumatic dilation, POEM, or LHM
landmarkTrials.finding: POEM and LHM superior to pneumatic dilation for all Eckardt components; treatment success 95%, 90%, 68% for POEM, LHM, and dilation respectively at 3 years
landmarkTrials.impact: Reinforced superiority of myotomy-based treatments; pneumatic dilation reserved for those not fit for intervention
landmarkTrials.reference: Kumagai K, Tsai JA, Thorell A, et al. Per-oral endoscopic myotomy for achalasia: are results comparable to laparoscopic Heller myotomy? Scand J Gastroenterol. 2015;50(5):505-512.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Laparoscopic Heller myotomy with partial fundoplication is first-line surgical therapy for all achalasia types in fit patients
evidenceTable.reference: ACG Clinical Guidelines for Achalasia (2020); RCTs and meta-analyses

evidenceTable.recommendation: POEM is as effective as Heller myotomy for symptom relief but carries higher post-procedural GERD risk
evidenceTable.reference: Werner YB et al. N Engl J Med. 2019; POEM-LHM trial

evidenceTable.recommendation: Pneumatic dilation is a safe and effective alternative for patients who are not surgical candidates or prefer non-surgical management
evidenceTable.reference: Boeckxstaens GE et al. N Engl J Med. 2011

evidenceTable.recommendation: Type II achalasia has the best treatment response; type III (spastic) responds best to POEM with longer myotomy
evidenceTable.reference: Chicago Classification v4.0; Pandolfino JE et al. Gastroenterology. 2021

evidenceTable.recommendation: Patients with achalasia should undergo surveillance endoscopy for esophageal cancer risk after 10-15 years of disease
evidenceTable.reference: Zaninotto G et al. Ann Surg. 2018; International Society for Diseases of the Esophagus guidelines

evidenceTable.recommendation: Intraoperative endoscopy or underwater air insufflation should be used to exclude mucosal perforation during myotomy
evidenceTable.reference: SAGES Guidelines for Achalasia (2023); expert opinion

evidenceTable.recommendation: In suspected Chagas-related achalasia in endemic areas (East Africa, South America), serologic testing should precede invasive procedures
evidenceTable.reference: WHO Chagas Disease Guidelines; Matsuda NM et al. Dis Esophagus. 2009

evidenceTable.recommendation: A fundoplication (Dor or Toupet) should be routinely added to Heller myotomy to prevent post-operative GERD
evidenceTable.reference: Meta-analysis: Rawlings A et al. Surg Endosc. 2012; 15 RCTs

evidenceTable.recommendation: Botulinum toxin injection should be reserved for patients unfit for surgery/dilation due to short duration of effect and risk of fibrosis
evidenceTable.reference: Leyden JE et al. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2014

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm:
Suspected Achalasia
  ├── Clinical suspicion: dysphagia to solids AND liquids, regurgitation, chest pain, Eckardt score
  ├── Upper GI Endoscopy (EGD)
  │   ├── Tight LES with resistance on passing scope, retained saliva/food → achalasia
  │   ├── Normal mucosa supports functional disorder
  │   ├── Mass at GEJ → pseudoachalasia (EUS, CT)
  │   └── Esophagitis, furrows, rings → consider GERD, EoE
  ├── Barium Swallow (if endoscopy non-diagnostic)
  │   ├── Bird-beak sign → achalasia
  │   ├── Corkscrew esophagus → DES
  │   └── Sigmoid esophagus → end-stage achalasia
  ├── High-Resolution Manometry (HRM) — Gold Standard
  │   ├── Type I: absent peristalsis, no pressurization → Classic achalasia
  │   ├── Type II: absent peristalsis, panesophageal pressurization → Best prognosis
  │   ├── Type III: absent peristalsis, spastic contractions → POEM preferred
  │   └── EGJ outflow obstruction → incomplete achalasia or mechanical
  ├── Chagas serology (if endemic area)
  └── Treatment selection
      ├── Fit for surgery: Heller myotomy + Dor fundoplication or POEM
      ├── Not fit: Pneumatic dilation (30-40mm balloon)
      └── Poor candidate: Botulinum toxin 100U

managementPearls.pearl: In any patient over 60 with rapid-onset dysphagia (<6 months) and weight loss >10%, pseudoachalasia from malignancy must be excluded. CT chest/abdomen and EUS with fine-needle aspiration of the GEJ are mandatory before myotomy. A failed myotomy that did not improve symptoms should prompt re-evaluation for pseudoachalasia.
managementPearls.evidence: Kahrilas PJ. JAMA. 2019; Ponds FA et al. Nat Rev Gastroenterol Hepatol. 2020

managementPearls.context: Myotomy length on the gastric side
managementPearls.pearl: The gastric myotomy must extend only 2.5-3 cm onto the gastric cardia. Extending >3 cm increases reflux risk dramatically without improving dysphagia relief. Use the angle of His and the sling fibers of the stomach as landmarks. Intraoperative endoscopy or transillumination helps identify the GEJ precisely.
managementPearls.evidence: Oelschlager BK et al. J Am Coll Surg. 2008; Zaninotto G et al. Ann Surg. 2011

managementPearls.pearl: Type III (spastic) achalasia requires a longer myotomy extending 8-12 cm above the GEJ to cover the spastic segment. POEM is preferred because it allows a tailored, longer myotomy. If performing Heller myotomy, extend the myotomy proximally beyond the spastic zone identified on HRM.
managementPearls.evidence: Pandolfino JE et al. Gastroenterology. 2021; Kumbhari V et al. Gastrointest Endosc. 2016

managementPearls.pearl: Up to 30% of patients after Heller myotomy with Dor fundoplication and 50% after POEM will have pathologic reflux. Routine pH monitoring at 6-12 months post-procedure is recommended regardless of symptoms. Asymptomatic reflux esophagitis can progress to Barrett's esophagus.
managementPearls.evidence: Werner YB et al. N Engl J Med. 2019; Schlottmann F et al. Surgery. 2017

managementPearls.context: Sigmoid esophagus — when to do esophagectomy
managementPearls.pearl: Patients with sigmoid (end-stage) esophagus and Eckardt score >6 after failed prior therapy should be considered for esophagectomy rather than repeat myotomy. The severely dilated, tortuous esophagus does not empty even with a patent myotomy. Transhiatal esophagectomy with gastric pull-up is the procedure of choice.
managementPearls.evidence: Devaney EJ et al. J Thorac Cardiovasc Surg. 2001; Loviscek MF et al. Ann Thorac Surg. 2014

managementPearls.context: Eckardt score use in resource-limited settings
managementPearls.pearl: The Eckardt score (dysphagia, regurgitation, chest pain, weight loss; each 0-3, total 0-12) is a powerful clinical tool that requires no equipment. Score >3 suggests need for intervention; >6 indicates severe disease. In Kenyan district hospitals without manometry, the Eckardt score plus barium swallow can guide management decisions.
managementPearls.evidence: Eckardt VF et al. Gut. 1992; Pandolfino JE. Curr Opin Gastroenterol. 2010

managementPearls.pearl: Graded pneumatic dilation starting with 30 mm balloon, then 35 mm, then 40 mm at 2-4 week intervals is safer than starting with 40 mm. The risk of perforation with 40 mm balloon in the first session is 3-5%. Use fluoroscopic guidance and inflate to 10-15 psi for 15-30 seconds.
managementPearls.evidence: Katsinelos P et al. Surg Endosc. 2006; Boeckxstaens GE. Gastroenterol Clin North Am. 2008

managementPearls.pearl: The relative risk of esophageal squamous cell carcinoma in achalasia is 16-33x that of the general population, with latency of 15-20 years. Annual surveillance endoscopy should begin 10-15 years after onset. In Kenya, where background esophageal cancer rates are high, consider initiating surveillance earlier.
managementPearls.evidence: Leeuwenburgh I et al. Am J Gastroenterol. 2006; Eckardt AJ et al. Dis Esophagus. 2014

managementPearls.pearl: Chagas disease (American trypanosomiasis) is not typically found in East Africa — rather it is endemic in South and Central America. In Kenyan patients, the more likely infectious mimic is HIV-associated esophageal dysmotility or tuberculosis causing mediastinal lymphadenopathy with pseudoachalasia. Chagas should be considered in patients from endemic areas with dilated cardiomyopathy and megacolon.
managementPearls.evidence: Matsuda NM et al. Dis Esophagus. 2009; WHO Chagas Disease Fact Sheet

managementPearls.pearl: Many achalasia patients present with >10% weight loss and protein-calorie malnutrition. Preoperative nutritional optimization with liquid nutritional supplements, nasoenteric feeding, or even total parenteral nutrition for 7-14 days before surgery reduces post-operative complications. Serum albumin <3.0 g/dL predicts poor wound healing and increased infection risk.
managementPearls.evidence: Zerbib F et al. Neurogastroenterol Motil. 2016; ASPEN Guidelines for Perioperative Nutrition (2020)

operativeNuances.procedure: Laparoscopic Heller Myotomy with Dor Fundoplication
operativeNuances.pitfall: Incomplete myotomy at the GEJ — the commonest cause of persistent dysphagia. Many surgeons fail to extend the myotomy far enough onto the gastric cardia or leave circular fibers intact at the junction.
operativeNuances.expertTip: After the myotomy, perform a 'dry swallow' test: pass a 56 Fr bougie or perform intraoperative endoscopy. The endoscope should pass through the GEJ with minimal resistance. Visible mucosal bulging through the myotomy site confirms completeness. Extend precisely to the sling fibers of the cardia but not beyond.

operativeNuances.procedure: Laparoscopic Heller Myotomy with Dor Fundoplication
operativeNuances.pitfall: Mucosal perforation during myotomy — occurs in 1-5% of cases, often when dividing the last circular muscle fibers over the thinned mucosa.
operativeNuances.expertTip: Always perform an underwater air insufflation test (submerge the myotomy site in saline and insufflate air through the NG tube). If bubbles appear, the defect must be repaired with interrupted 4-0 absorbable sutures and the Dor fundoplication placed over the repair. Do not convert to Nissen fundoplication — the wrap must cover the repair.

operativeNuances.procedure: Laparoscopic Heller Myotomy with Dor Fundoplication
operativeNuances.pitfall: Vagus nerve injury during hiatal dissection — the anterior and posterior vagal trunks run intimately along the esophagus. Injury causes gastroparesis and dumping syndrome.
operativeNuances.expertTip: Identify the vagus nerves during dissection of the gastroesophageal junction. The anterior vagus is visible as a white cord-like structure on the anterior esophageal wall at the GEJ. Dissect it off the esophagus before the myotomy and retract it safely to the right. The posterior vagus is protected by staying anterior during myotomy.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Incorrect tunnel orientation in the submucosal plane leads to mucosal injury, incomplete myotomy, or entry into the wrong layer.
operativeNuances.expertTip: Always maintain the tunnel within the submucosal plane between the mucosal layer (visible as blue-tinged on the 'roof') and the circular muscle layer (white, appearing on the 'floor'). Use a tapered transparent cap and confirm orientation by noting the longitudinal muscle fibers. The myotomy should be in the posterior position (5-7 o'clock) to reduce reflux.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Uncontrolled bleeding from gastric vessels during extension of the myotomy onto the gastric cardia — the gastric submucosa has larger vessels than the esophagus.
operativeNuances.expertTip: Use the coagulation-grasping forceps for prophylactic coaptive coagulation of visible vessels before cutting. When entering the gastric side, the tunnel widens and vessels increase — use a distal attachment cap to compress tissue for better hemostasis. Switch to a soft coagulation current (80W, effect 4) on the tips of the forceps.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Hemorrhage from mediastinal vessels during blind transhiatal dissection — the aorta, azygos vein, and bronchial arteries are at risk.
operativeNuances.expertTip: Stay in the correct areolar tissue plane immediately adjacent to the esophageal wall. Use a headlight and long DeBakey forceps for careful blunt dissection. If bleeding occurs, pack the mediastinum with a laparotomy sponge for 5 minutes. For significant bleeding, convert to a right thoracotomy. Preoperative CT with 3D reconstruction of mediastinal anatomy is invaluable.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Tracheal or left main bronchus injury during mid-esophageal dissection — catastrophic complication with high mortality.
operativeNuances.expertTip: The tracheal bifurcation lies immediately anterior to the mid-esophagus. Always maintain a posterior dissection plane. Palpate the endotracheal tube intraoperatively. In re-operative cases or after previous myotomy, consider a combined thoracoscopic approach. If injury occurs, immediate primary repair with an intercostal muscle flap coverage and postoperative ventilation is required.

prognosis.overallMortality: Procedure-related mortality for Heller myotomy is <0.5%; for POEM, <0.1%. Esophagectomy for end-stage carries 3-5% mortality. Pneumatic dilation perforation rate 1-5% with associated mortality of <0.5%.
prognosis.morbidity: Post-operative GERD 20-30% after Heller myotomy with Dor fundoplication; 35-50% after POEM. Mucosal perforation 1-5%. Recurrent dysphagia 10-20% at 5-10 years.
prognosis.recurrence: Recurrent dysphagia in 10-20% within 10 years. Treatment failure higher in type III achalasia. Need for re-intervention in 15-25% of dilated patients. After esophagectomy, anastomotic stricture in 25-40% requiring dilation.
prognosis.survivalData: 10-year survival >90% for treated achalasia. Esophageal cancer develops in 2-8% after 15-20 years of achalasia (standardized incidence ratio 16-33). Annual surveillance endoscopy starting 10-15 years after diagnosis is recommended.
prognosis.landmarkReference: Boeckxstaens GE, et al. N Engl J Med. 2011; Werner YB, et al. N Engl J Med. 2019; Eckardt AJ, et al. Dis Esophagus. 2014

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Severe dysphagia/megaesophagus -> SEVERE; mild-moderate symptoms -> NON-SEVERE

history_questions.question: Do you have difficulty swallowing both solids and liquids, or just solids?
Does food ever come back up into your mouth after eating?
Do you have chest pain or discomfort behind your breastbone when swallowing?
Do you cough or choke at night while sleeping?
Have you lost weight unintentionally in the past few months?
Do you feel like food gets stuck in your chest?
Have you had heartburn that does not respond to antacids or omeprazole?
Do you avoid eating in front of others because of embarrassment?
Do you drink busaa, chang'aa, or other homemade alcohol?
Have you ever been diagnosed with Chagas disease?
Do you have a family member with achalasia or swallowing problems?
How long does it take you to finish a meal?
Do you need to drink water to push food down?
Have you ever had food stuck so badly you could not swallow saliva?
Do you have frequent chest infections or pneumonia?
Do you eat ugali or other dry starchy foods — do they make symptoms worse?
Have you ever had an endoscopy (camera down the throat)?
Do you smoke cigarettes or use tobacco?
Have you been treated for tuberculosis in the past?
Does your voice become hoarse after eating or drinking?
Do you feel like you have a lump in your throat when swallowing?
Have you ever lived in or traveled to South America or rural areas with Chagas disease?
Do you have difficulty belching or passing gas?
Have you ever been diagnosed with autoimmune disease such as lupus, scleroderma, or Sjögren's syndrome?
Do you wake up at night coughing with a sour or bitter taste in your mouth?
Have you had any prior surgeries on your esophagus, stomach, or diaphragm?
Do you have any numbness, tingling, or weakness in your arms or legs?
Have you noticed your food taking longer to go down over the past months or years?
Do you avoid certain foods because they get stuck more easily?
Have you ever been told you have a dilated esophagus on X-ray or other tests?
Do you drink a lot of water or other fluids during meals to help food pass?
Have you ever had food come out of your nose when trying to swallow?
Do you have chronic bad breath that does not improve with brushing?
Have you ever had an esophageal manometry test?
Do you feel bubbling or gurgling sensations in your chest after eating?
Have you noticed that cold beverages make your swallowing worse?
Are you able to sleep flat, or do you need to prop yourself up?
Do you have difficulty swallowing your own saliva on some days?
Have you had any dental problems from frequent regurgitation?
Do you experience hiccups frequently after meals?

examination_findings.finding: Weight loss evident on examination
Signs of aspiration — crackles in lung bases on auscultation
Cachexia and muscle wasting in advanced disease
Dehydration signs — dry oral mucosa, reduced skin turgor
Halitosis from fermented food in dilated esophagus
Suprasternal fullness from dilated esophagus
Gurgling sound on chest auscultation after swallowing
Clubbing — rare, suggests chronic aspiration
Pallor from nutritional deficiencies
Fever — if aspiration pneumonia present
Tachycardia from dehydration or infection
Reduced air entry at lung bases if effusion from aspiration
Epigastric tenderness — may be present
Visible peristalsis — left neck (rare)
Dental enamel erosion from frequent regurgitation of acid
Signs of Horner's syndrome (rare - advanced mediastinal involvement)
Asterixis if concomitant liver disease from alcohol use
Dry, cracked lips and oral mucosa from chronic dehydration
Tracheal deviation in massive megaesophagus
Dullness to percussion at the right lung base (aspiration changes)
Palpable left supraclavicular node (Virchow's node) — if malignancy has developed
Neck swelling after drinking water (visible dilated esophagus in the neck)
Vocal cord palsy (recurrent laryngeal nerve compression from megaesophagus)
Poor peripheral perfusion from chronic malnutrition
Skin changes — dermatitis, hyperkeratosis from vitamin deficiencies
Angular stomatitis from nutritional deficiencies (iron, B vitamins)
Thyroid gland examination — exclude goiter causing extrinsic compression
Check for parotid swelling (rare association with eating disorders and bulimia)
Foot and ankle edema from protein malnutrition

riskFactors.note: Endemic in parts of East Africa, South America; destroys myenteric plexus
Delayed presentation, poor access to manometry

subtypes.name: Type I (Classic) Achalasia
subtypes.management: Laparoscopic Heller myotomy with Dor fundoplication. POEM as alternative. Pneumatic dilation for poor surgical candidates.

subtypes.name: Type II (Panesophageal Pressurization) Achalasia
subtypes.management: Laparoscopic Heller myotomy + Dor fundoplication. POEM. Pneumatic dilation as alternative.

subtypes.name: Type III (Spastic) Achalasia
subtypes.management: POEM preferred (longer myotomy). Laparoscopic Heller myotomy with extended myotomy. Medical therapy (calcium channel blockers, nitrates) for poor surgical candidates.

mimics.whyMimic: Gastric cardia cancer infiltrating the LES can mimic achalasia
Presents with dysphagia and can appear similar on imaging
Young patients with dysphagia and food impaction
Chest pain and dysphagia can mimic achalasia
Can present with dysphagia and esophageal dilatation; absence of peristalsis on manometry may be confused with achalasia
Progressive dysphagia initially may be mistaken for achalasia
Extrinsic compression of the distal esophagus by TB lymph nodes can cause dysphagia and mimic achalasia

laboratory.name: Chagas serology
laboratory.indication: In endemic areas including East Africa

imaging.name: Barium Swallow
imaging.indication: Bird beak appearance, esophageal dilatation, sigmoid esophagus

imaging.name: High-Resolution Manometry (HRM)
imaging.indication: Gold standard — Chicago Classification types I-III

imaging.name: Upper GI Endoscopy (EGD)
imaging.indication: Rule out pseudoachalasia, evaluate for esophagitis, retained food

imaging.name: CT Chest with contrast
imaging.indication: If pseudoachalasia suspected — look for mass at GEJ

resuscitation: NBM — nil by mouth if severe dysphagia or aspiration risk
IV fluids — Normal Saline 1 L 8-hourly if dehydrated
Nutritional assessment — consider NG tube or TPN if severely malnourished
Aspiration precautions — elevate head of bed 30-45 degrees at all times
conservative: Dietary modification: semisolid foods, thickened liquids, small frequent meals
Avoid: dry foods (ugali, bread), carbonated drinks, alcohol
Sleep with head elevated to prevent nocturnal regurgitation
Nitrates (isosorbide dinitrate) or calcium channel blockers (nifedipine) sublingual before meals — temporary relief

surgical.description: Laparoscopic Heller myotomy with Dor fundoplication
surgical.indication: All fit patients with achalasia, especially type III
surgical.step2: Identify GEJ and gastroesophageal fat pad
surgical.step4: Myotomy: 6 cm above GEJ on esophagus, 2.5-3 cm onto gastric cardia
surgical.step5: Ensure complete myotomy — visible mucosal bulging
surgical.step7: Check for mucosal perforation — underwater air insufflation
surgical.step8: Close crura if hiatal hernia present

nonOperative: POEM (Peroral Endoscopic Myotomy) — preferred for type III, spastic disorders
Pneumatic dilation (30-40 mm balloon) — graded dilation, 30-50% require repeat
Botox injection (100 U) — for poor surgical candidates, effect lasts 6-12 months

general: Water-soluble contrast swallow on day 1 — check for leak
If no leak: clear fluids day 1, soft diet day 2, regular diet day 7-14
Proton pump inhibitor for 4 weeks postop
diet_advancement: Day 1: Clear fluids (water, tea, clear soup) after negative swallow study
Day 2-3: Soft diet (porridge, yoghurt, mashed potatoes, uji)
Day 4-7: Semisolid diet (soft rice, cooked vegetables, eggs)
Week 2+: Regular diet as tolerated — eat slowly, chew well
discharge_criteria: No evidence of leak on swallow study

pathways.criteria: Eckardt score 3-6, no megaesophagus, surgical candidate
pathways.treatment: Laparoscopic Heller myotomy with Dor fundoplication. POEM as alternative. Pneumatic dilation if surgery contraindicated.

pathways.criteria: Eckardt score >6, megaesophagus, sigmoid esophagus, failed prior therapy
pathways.treatment: Esophagectomy (transhiatal or Ivor Lewis) for end-stage. Repeat myotomy or POEM if prior myotomy failed. Botox injection for poor surgical candidates.

operative_templates.procedure: Laparoscopic Heller Myotomy with Dor Fundoplication
operative_templates.indication: Achalasia Cardia (Types I-III) — failed medical therapy
steps: Patient supine, split-leg position, reverse Trendelenburg
5-port laparoscopic setup: umbilical camera, left/right upper quadrant, subxiphoid, left flank
Retract left lobe of liver medially with liver retractor
Divide gastrohepatic ligament, identify right crus
Identify and preserve vagus nerves
Divide phrenoesophageal ligament carefully
Clear gastroesophageal fat pad
Longitudinal myotomy: start 6 cm above GEJ on esophageal body
Extend myotomy 2.5-3 cm onto gastric cardia
Ensure all circular muscle fibers divided — visible mucosal bulge
Dor fundoplication: anterior 180-degree wrap covering myotomy
Test mucosal integrity: underwater insufflation via NG tube
Close crura if hiatal hernia present
Place drain near myotomy site optionally

operative_templates.indication: End-stage achalasia with sigmoid esophagus, failed prior therapy
steps: Supine position, neck extended and turned right
Midline laparotomy or laparoscopic
Mobilize stomach preserving right gastroepiploic arcade
Transhiatal dissection of esophagus from mediastinum
Left cervical incision along sternocleidomastoid
Deliver cervical esophagus, anastomose to gastric conduit
Place feeding jejunostomy
Place chest drain in mediastinum

watchFor: worsening dysphagia after years of stability

dischargeCriteria: Tolerating soft diet without dysphagia or choking
No evidence of esophageal perforation on swallow study (if myotomy)
Able to sleep with head elevated safely
Patient and family educated on diet modifications and aspiration precautions

patient_education.explanation: Achalasia is a condition where the muscle at the bottom of your food pipe (esophagus) fails to relax when you swallow. The food pipe also loses its ability to push food down. This causes food to get stuck in your chest, which can lead to regurgitation, chest pain, coughing at night, and weight loss.
patient_education.warningSigns: Go to the hospital immediately if: you cannot swallow even your saliva, you cough up food that has been in the esophagus for hours, you have high fever with cough (may be aspiration pneumonia), or you vomit blood.
patient_education.activity: Sleep with the head of your bed elevated 30-45 degrees using blocks or extra pillows. Avoid lying down for at least 2 hours after eating. You can resume normal activities 2-3 weeks after surgery. Avoid heavy lifting for 6 weeks.
patient_education.diet: Eat small, frequent meals. Take your time eating — chew food thoroughly. Drink water during meals to help food pass. Avoid dry foods like ugali, bread, and chapati in the first weeks. Avoid alcohol, carbonated drinks, and very cold beverages. Good foods: porridge (uji), yoghurt, soup, mashed potatoes, soft rice, eggs, cooked vegetables.
patient_education.medication: After surgery: Take omeprazole 20mg daily for 4 weeks to protect the esophagus. Painkillers as prescribed. If you had Botox injection, it lasts 6-12 months — symptoms may return.
patient_education.followUp: Return to clinic: 2 weeks after surgery for wound check. 3 months for symptom assessment. Repeat manometry and barium swallow may be needed if symptoms recur. Annual follow-up for long-standing achalasia (increased cancer risk).

before_discharge: Educate patient on head-of-bed elevation and aspiration precautions
Dietician review — semisolid, high-nutrition diet plan

after_discharge.action: Surgical clinic review — wound check, symptom assessment
Clinical assessment — Eckardt score, need for further intervention
Barium swallow or manometry if symptoms persist
Final review — consider endoscopy for surveillance if long-standing
Surveillance for esophageal carcinoma in chronic achalasia >10-15 years

when_to_return: Inability to swallow saliva or liquids
High fever with cough or shortness of breath
Return of swallowing difficulty after initial improvement
